Failure here invalidates the positioning.","R1.7":"None of the three prior-work categories are grounded with specific citations; coverage gaps (e.g., omission of agent-specific schedulers or persistent-execution systems that may already handle dynamic graphs) and attribution accuracy are central to the claim that no prior system targets this scheduling unit."},"clarification_needed":false,"clarification_question":""}

rationale.R1.1: The contribution claim ('previously unaddressed point in the design space') and the gap statement anchored to the (reasoning-step, tool-call) scheduling unit must be evaluated for accuracy and sharpness; if the framing is imprecise the entire positioning fails.
rationale.R1.2: Each of the three contrast arguments rests on specific premises about what prior systems cannot do (stateless requests, static DAGs, IDL-declared call graphs); the logical chain from those premises to the differentiation conclusion must hold across all three threads and be mutually consistent.
rationale.R1.4: This is the load-bearing dimension for the delegation: assessing whether the novelty delta vs. LM serving, workflow orchestration, and RPC runtimes is real, complete, and not inadvertently covered by existing work (e.g., Durable Execution frameworks with dynamic task emission, speculative decoding schedulers). Failure here invalidates the positioning.
rationale.R1.7: None of the three prior-work categories are grounded with specific citations; coverage gaps (e.g., omission of agent-specific schedulers or persistent-execution systems that may already handle dynamic graphs) and attribution accuracy are central to the claim that no prior system targets this scheduling unit.
